question.text: Which AIF category can be structured as open-ended, and what types of funds does it include?

answer.text: Category I -- Venture Capital Funds and Social Venture Funds
Category II -- Private Equity Funds and Debt Funds
Category III -- Hedge Funds and PIPE Funds
All three categories can be open-ended or close-ended

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Only AIF Category III can be structured as either open-ended or close-ended. Category III includes Hedge Funds and PIPE (Private Investment in Public Equity) Funds. Categories I and II must be close-ended with a minimum tenure of 3 years. **The Trap:** "All three categories can be open-ended or close-ended" sounds logical because mutual funds offer both open and closed structures. However, AIFs are more restrictive -- only Category III has the flexibility of open-ended structure, reflecting its need for liquidity to execute trading strategies. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-B frequently tests the structural differences across AIF categories. The open-ended option for Category III is a key differentiator from Categories I and II. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Category III's ability to be open-ended means clients can potentially exit without waiting for maturity. This liquidity feature, combined with hedge fund-style strategies, makes Category III suitable for sophisticated investors who want alternative strategies with some liquidity.
